rationale: The canonical refactoring kata: deeply nested name-string conditionals, magic numbers, and the Item-must-not-change constraint. Well-known good refactoring path and fast golden-master tests, so apply+verify is cheap and reliable (unlike nx).

smells: Primitive Obsession (behavior keyed by the name string)
targets.expected_family: Decompose Conditional + Extract Function first (guard clauses; extract per-branch quality/sellIn updates; name the magic numbers). Then optionally Replace Conditional with Polymorphism / Strategy per item type.
targets.judgment: Golden-master tests are green -> refactor in small behavior-preserving steps, running tests after each. Constraint: do NOT modify Item -- so any polymorphism must be a SEPARATE per-type updater/strategy (or Map<name, updater>), never an Item subclass. Clarity-first: Extract + Decompose removes most pain; full polymorphism earns its keep mainly because the type set grows (the kata README adds 'Conjured'). A good coach reaches Extract/Decompose first and reserves the hierarchy for the growing-variance case.

targets.expected_family: Judge the proposed subclass-per-type against the constraint: subclassing Item is off-limits, so the constraint-respecting form is a per-type updater/strategy hierarchy or a Map<name, updater>. Recommend Decompose Conditional + Extract Function first regardless.
targets.judgment: Tests the over/under-engineering balance AND the do-not-modify-Item constraint together. A good coach: (a) flags that subclassing Item is off-limits, (b) offers a constraint-respecting shape (separate strategy/updater, not an Item subclass), (c) recommends Extract/Decompose as the safe first move and reserves full polymorphism for when the type set actually grows (Conjured etc.) -- neither dogmatically refusing nor blindly endorsing the hierarchy.
